Other Formats

  • RevealJS

Week 11 — Adult Sex Ratio from Camera Traps

SNR 690 — Contemporary Approaches to Quantitative Methods

1 Entry Ticket

On your notecard, write answers to:

  1. What is the formula for adult sex ratio (ASR)?
  2. From Week 10: What is the core problem that occupancy models solve?
  3. If 40% of your camera-trap sightings cannot be sexed, how might that bias your ASR estimate?

. . .

Compare your answers with your neighbor — do you agree?

2 Why Adult Sex Ratio Matters

Ungulate management depends on population structure

  • ASR influences harvest quotas, population growth projections, and habitat management
  • Sex-biased browsing pressure affects forest regeneration
  • Camera traps are cost-effective but imperfect sensors

. . .

Important

The problem: Not every sighting can be sexed. Unknown-sex sightings may bias ASR estimates if ignored or handled incorrectly.

3 The ASR Equation

\[\text{ASR} = \frac{N_{\text{males}}}{N_{\text{males}} + N_{\text{females}}}\]

. . .

  • ASR = 0.5 → equal numbers of males and females
  • ASR < 0.5 → female-biased (common in polygynous ungulates)
  • ASR > 0.5 → male-biased

. . .

Key question: What do we do with sightings where sex is unknown?

4 Study Design Snapshot

Wohlfahrt et al. (2026)

  • 24 camera traps, Austrian Alps
  • 20,250 camera-days
  • 4,110 adult/subadult sightings
  • 436 unknown-sex (~8.9%)
  • Jan 2020 – May 2022
  • Focus: weeks 9–17

Camera placement:

  • Along wildlife trails
  • Maximizes detections
  • But may introduce placement bias

Sighting definition:

  • ≥ 5 min between images = new sighting

5 Three Approaches to ASR

flowchart LR
    A[Camera Trap\nSightings] --> B{Sex known?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Naive ASR]
    B -->|No| D[Predict sex\nwith GAM]
    D --> E[Predicted ASR]
    A --> F[Detection\nhistories]
    F --> G[RN Occupancy\nModel]
    G --> H[Occupancy ASR]

6 Approach 1: Naive ASR

Simple: Use only sightings where sex is identified

\[\text{ASR}_{\text{naive}} = \frac{N_{\text{males, known}}}{N_{\text{males, known}} + N_{\text{females, known}}}\]

. . .

Assumption: Unknown-sex sightings are missing completely at random (MCAR)

. . .

When this fails:

  • Males may be easier to identify (antlers) → unknowns skew female
  • Camera placement may favor one sex
  • Unknown rate varies by season or camera location

7 Approach 2: Predicted ASR (GAM)

Idea: Predict sex for unknown sightings using a statistical model

. . .

Model: Generalized Additive Model (binomial, logit link)

\[\text{logit}(P(\text{male})) = f_1(\text{day\_of\_year}) + f_2(\text{hour}) + s(\text{camera\_id}) + f_3(\text{dist\_road} \times \text{day\_of\_year})\]

. . .

Prediction rule: If \(P(\text{male}) > 0.5\) → classify as male; otherwise female

. . .

Performance: Precision ≈ 75.24%; Deviance explained ≈ 29.5%

8 Approach 3: Occupancy-Based ASR (Royle–Nichols)

Key insight: Detection probability depends on abundance

\[P(\text{detect} \geq 1 \mid N, r) = 1 - (1-r)^N\]

. . .

  • \(N\) = latent abundance at a site (Poisson distributed)
  • \(r\) = per-individual detection probability

. . .

For ASR: Fit RN model separately for males and females

\[\text{ASR}_{\text{occupancy}} = \frac{\bar{N}_{\text{male}}}{\bar{N}_{\text{male}} + \bar{N}_{\text{female}}}\]

. . .

Requires: Detection histories (sites × weekly replicates), closure assumption

9 Quick Poll

ImportantThink Before the Presentation

Which approach do you think gives the most reliable ASR estimate? Why?

  1. Naive — simplicity is a feature, not a bug

  2. Predicted — more data is always better

  3. Occupancy RN — accounts for the detection process

  4. It depends on the study design

Hold your answer — revisit after the presentation

10 Student Presentation

10.1 Wohlfahrt et al. (2026)

Red deer adult sex ratio: comparing three approaches using camera trap data

Journal of Wildlife Management 90:e70139

. . .

Discussion roles assigned:

Role Job
Facilitator Keep discussion flowing
Skeptic Challenge assumptions
Scribe Capture key points
Assumption-Checker Flag statistical assumptions

11 Discussion Prompts

After the presentation:

  1. The GAM’s prediction precision was ~75%. Is that good enough?
  2. Cameras were placed along trails. How might this bias ASR?
  3. Why focus on weeks 9–17? What assumption does this satisfy?

. . .

From the paper:

“Our hypothesis was that these advanced statistical methods reveal a potential bias in the estimation of red deer ASR in a naive approach directly from raw camera trap data.” (Wohlfahrt et al. 2026)

12 Jigsaw Activity

Expert phase (7 min):

Your group studies ONE approach in depth:

Group A Group B Group C
Naive ASR Predicted (GAM) Occupancy (RN)

For your method:

  1. Write the key equation on the whiteboard
  2. List 2–3 assumptions
  3. Identify 1–2 failure scenarios
  4. Prepare a 2-min teach-back

. . .

Teach-back (7 min): Regroup into mixed teams — teach your method!

13 Method Comparison Summary

Feature Naive Predicted (GAM) Occupancy (RN)
Uses unknown sightings ✗ ✓ (predicted) ✗ (separate model)
Accounts for detection bias ✗ Partially ✓
Data requirement Low Moderate High (repeated visits)
Analytical complexity Low Moderate High
Key assumption MCAR unknowns Correct classification Closure + Poisson abundance

14 Choose-Your-Own-Method

Scenario 1 — Tropical Primates: 50 cameras, 40% unknowns, cameras at fruiting trees, 6 months of data.

Scenario 2 — Prairie Elk: 15 trail cameras, 5% unknowns, minimal stats capacity.

Scenario 3 — Island Invasive Deer: 100 cameras in grid, 20% unknowns, skilled analyst on team.

. . .

For your scenario: Which method? Why? What are the risks?

Write your group’s answer on the poster → gallery walk!

15 Structured Debate

Motion: “Naive ASR is sufficient for most wildlife management applications.”

. . .

Side A: “Keep It Simple” Side B: “Model It Right”
Prepare 2 arguments (2 min) Prepare 2 arguments (2 min)
Present (2 min) Present (2 min)

. . .

Free rebuttal (2 min) → Instructor synthesis (2 min)

16 What Did the Paper Find?

Year Naive ASR Predicted ASR Occupancy ASR
2020 0.40 (0.34–0.46) 0.44 (0.39–0.49) 0.40 (0.34–0.47)
2021 0.40 (0.31–0.49) 0.46 (0.39–0.54) 0.36 (0.36–0.36)
2022 0.42 (0.35–0.50) 0.48 (0.41–0.56) 0.45 (0.44–0.46)

. . .

Bottom line: Under these conditions (~8.9% unknowns, trail cameras), all three methods gave comparable results.

. . .

But: This does NOT mean naive is always fine. When unknowns are >20–30% or cameras are non-randomly placed, advanced methods become essential.

17 Concept Map Activity

Draw connections between these terms:

ASR · naive · predicted · GAM · occupancy · RN · detection probability · latent abundance · bootstrap CI · camera placement · unknowns

. . .

Label your arrows: “assumes,” “accounts for,” “biased by,” “estimates”

. . .

Muddiest point: Write the one thing still unclear to you.

18 Minute Paper

On your notecard:

  1. Most important insight from today?
  2. Which approach for YOUR research system?
  3. One question for the workshop?

19 Up Next: Workshop (Session B)

Hands-on R exercises:

  • Exercise A: Naive ASR + bootstrap CI
  • Exercise B: GAM sex prediction + confusion matrix
  • Exercise C: Royle–Nichols occupancy model

. . .

Packages you’ll need:

install.packages(c("mgcv", "boot", "unmarked",
                    "dplyr", "ggplot2", "tidyr"))

. . .

Let’s code! 🦌📸
